speaker: interviewer
text: --January 11, 1996. OK. I have a couple more questions about when you were in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- were you allowed to keep all of your belongings that your family brought with you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I best recall, yes. And of course, we didn't bring any utensils.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure everybody had the toothbrushes and combs and things like that. But utensils-- so we had these tin bowls--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --large spoon, and a cup that was issued-- that was issued to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your parents-- they were allowed to spend time together. They weren't kept apart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- they-- they-- they could spend together. I mean, people would meet, and they would talk. But primarily, people were depressed, in fear, not knowing what to do. I mean, if you had a job, something to do at least, you know. [? If ?] your mind was wandering every minute, what's going to happen to me? What's going to be? So people were very, very depressed. My father and mother didn't have that much time together because my father was working. I mean, you know, with a thousand-some-odd people-- I don't know how many of them were children. Always somebody was sick, and people needed to be at the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of the people that came there in your group, most of them left there healthy? Nobody got--

speaker: interviewer
text: --sick, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I mean, some people obviously had to get sick. I mean, people get sick, you know, under normal circumstances. And being deprived of food and proper medication, I'm sure people got sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure we almost had one or two deaths, also. I mean, just [INAUDIBLE], you know. I-- but I really can't tell you for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not necessarily--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not because of specific abuse.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How many-- did the majority of the people there have some kind of work that they could do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were people who were craftsmen. And craftspeople repaired shoes, you know, who were able to-- I mean, the craftspeople generally bring their tools with them, you know, just like people-- craftspeople just do-- do that. And of course, those people were mending shoes, and women were, of course, fixing hems in clothing and shortening. And people were selling-- selling clothing that were-- made the lining for warm-up jackets or coats. My father, having been a pediatrician in a small town, which was mainly in a agrarian society, a lot of times got paid not in money, but in kind. And he has this leather vest. I guess it was a sheepskin, got-- it was very well embroidered and what have you and nicely done. Some Hungarian peasant bartered him with that. That probably saved his life. I mean, he wore it under his clothes, and I still have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still have it. It's an old, old leather vest. And then subsequently my mother knitted some sleeves to it and I wore it, but I took the sleeves off. I still have it as a souvenir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. So you and your sister would mostly just play together during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We play. We try to learn some Hebrew songs. I said we learned how to dance the hora. We were planning to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's [? aliyah. ?] We're going to the-- the land, the Promised Land. And we learned songs, and we sang. We played. Really, we played. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I look back-- of course, it was no fun being there. But I look back, it's the same as-- as-- as the children in the other ghettos. We're not starving. I mean, for children, young children, you know, it's boring. You're not going to school. You just-- you have to have something to do. And children are very, very resilient in finding games to play.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we played games. We played engineer. We played, like I said, we played soccer, the boys. And-- but I don't remember playing with my sister. I just remember playing with the boys and trying to learn a little bit of Polish from those orphans that were with us. And you know, it was a very long day. And you get up, and you wait for-- we were exhausted by the time the appell was over, and you haven't done anything. And of course, you felt very bad for the people next door who were working. They were building. They were doing. They were constructing all the time for more people to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Russians-- were they Russian Jews, or were they Russian prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think they were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] The Dutch were Jews, next to us. Did you want to ask me anything, because there's something that I--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, go--

speaker: interviewee
text: --meant to say when I-- I planned this-- this out, and I just-- I'm not saying it the right way. If you recall, I mentioned that people would write their names or carve their names into the beds, the triple-decker beds. I mean, fate is-- truth is stranger than fiction. Three days or four days after we left, they were moving the people out of the East.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they brought my mother's mother and my father's mother to the very same compound in Bergen-Belsen where we were. And of course, my father's mother was in the B barracks, because her name was Berger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: In exact same place where we were. And we knew we were going to Switzerland. So we left for Switzerland, and she said, I know that my children are safe, and now I can die in peace. And she died in Bergen-Belsen. That's my-- my father's mother, my paternal grandmother. My maternal grandmother, who was there also, she survived the war, and she went back to Yugoslavia and subsequently to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, the thing about putting your name down, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had-- you had put your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother did?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had put down, as-- as everybody else, you know, did. Who knows--

speaker: interviewer
text: She had just written her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because, you know, somehow, yes, did you see-- well, we know that on such-and-such that so-and-so was still alive because of the date and things. And that's how people would know that people lived or were here or passed through. And later on, when people and families were reunited, this was one of the ways that stories got around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. So when you went to Switzerland, you guys-- your-- your family was in a hotel together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For six months?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there-- well, we were-- well, much longer. We were in Caux until the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we went to a place called Clarens, C-L-A-R-E-N-S, which is right down on the Lake Geneva. It's between a town of of Vevey and Montreux. And we were at a place called Maribeau, M-A-R-I-B-E-A-U. Again, my father was a physician. Now, stranger than that is what I mentioned about my grandparents. My wife's family was with us in the very same concentration camp at the very same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her-- her brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, your-- I'm sorry, your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her mother and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were with us also not only in Caux, but they also were with us in the other part of Switzerland. When the war was over and Tito asked for people to come back, my father said, hell no. He said, I don't like fascism. I don't like communism. I don't like any -isms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a free man who likes to have free thought. And I survived this war not to go back to something. So when Tito asked to go back, my dad just refused to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-in-law, on the other hand, took his family and went back to Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's an interesting coincidence [? there. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. So we then were there during the summer of '40, part of the summer of '40. I'm sorry, I'm getting the dates mixed up. It was in '45 or '46. Maybe it was late '45. '46 we were-- early '46 we were sent to another place where-- called Aigle, A-I-G-L-E, and we were the only Jews there. It was Russian workers and their families, who had been, you know, forced laborers in Germany who were-- and brought to Switzerland. And we were there. We were the only Jews-- my dad and my mother, my sister, and I. And we got along very well with the administrative staff, the Swiss officers. And to this date, I mean, just the last year the last one died. We were constantly in contact with them. Every New Year and holidays that we would-- we kept in touch with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a question.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started learning French.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, excuse me. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: The time that you were in Switzerland, you were all together as a group in the first place--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you were in.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Caux, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you moved down-- I'm sorry, what's the name of the town to which you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Clarens. First is Clarens.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you still together with the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: A group, a group. Not a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were moving-- trying to move people around. The war was over now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who was trying-- who was, like, taking control of your situation in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Swiss government.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Swiss government.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you were DP. You were a displaced person. And you were stateless, displaced person with no passports.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what kinds of-- do you know what kinds of places some of the other families were sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all the places were similar, all the same. Now, after some time, we went to a place in a town called Teritet, T-E-R-I-T-E-T, also in the Swiss part, also not far from Montreux. And here we went to a home, which also had been, once upon a time, a hotel. We were under the Swiss administration, but of course there were no cops. There were no police, no army. The war was over. And they received money from the Red Cross. And some people were given permission to live there and work in the economy, and the others were forced to live there. My sister and I were, as the other children were, allowed to go to the local school. So it's my first time I'm going to school was in 1946 in French.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you nine?

speaker: interviewee
text: '46, I was nine. Actually, it was-- I'm sorry. It must have been January of-- or the the end of January '46 I was eight. I was eight, [INAUDIBLE] the very beginning [INAUDIBLE] or December of '45. And I went to school, and it was a two-room school, first and second grade one room, third, fourth, and fifth grade in the other room. It was a nice school. And I have to tell you, they took us in. The children were so nice and so friendly. They took us in. And we still have friends from those days we'd gone to school with, and we keep in contact, especially my sister. And we came and went, and we're fine. And we [INAUDIBLE]. My dad, of course, worked, not in the economy, but worked as a physician at the place. Now, the Joint Distribution Agency and the Red Cross began publishing lists of people who survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a man named Mr. Havas, H-A-V-A-S, also his first name, just like my dad's, Imre, I-M-R-E, was looking through the list, and he sees IM Berger, wife and two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, is this my dear friend and schoolmate, or is it somebody with the same initials? What am I going to do? I am going to send a check, and I think it was for $50, drawn on the Irving Trust Company at 225 Broadway. I will sign my name. In those days, you didn't have the person's name printed on the-- on the check. So he said, if this is my former roommate, he will recognize my signature. He will be intelligent enough to find a way to get in touch with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sure enough--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was us. It was him. My dad writes-- wrote a letter, Dear Mr. Irving, not knowing what the Irving Trust Company was. And the banks were a little different. When Mr. Havas walked into that branch, people say, good morning, Mr. Havas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's like people knew who you were in those days. People that worked in the bank didn't work there three weeks, and they were sent out to an ATM machine. And this correspondence was started. And Mr. Havas had two brothers, and they were raised by the grandparents. They were orphan-- raised by the grandparents and were very, very close friends of my father, dear friends of my father. And one of them was in the motion picture business. And he was the European rep for RKO, and he was stationed in Paris. So of course, contacts were-- and he got in contact with us. He came to Switzerland. You know, he has an American passport. He came to Switzerland to see us where we were living in Teritet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was Mr. Havas's brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: His younger brother, Mike, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who also was friends with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had also--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who also--

speaker: interviewer
text: --good friends with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was good-- I mean, they [INAUDIBLE] came from [INAUDIBLE], three-- these three brothers came from [INAUDIBLE], I mean, the same town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they left-- had they left before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Havas, if you had ever seen the movie he took that last boat, that last plane. He just got out just in time, and he got here just in time to sort of-- to save himself. And his two other brothers were-- one was working for Disney and for RKO, so he was an American citizen. And the third one, Eugene, he was a financial advisor to FDR. So he had been here many, many, many years before the older-- the oldest. And of course, contacts were made. And it was almost impossible to come to the United States then. I mean, the-- the quota for Hungarian or Yugoslav Jews were almost nil, and it was very difficult. So my dad decided that he had had his fill of Europe. He was a Zionist. And he could not get, obviously, a-- a piece of paper from the British that we could go to Palestine. I assume you-- you know that, that  it was such terrific restriction on Jewish immigration to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So didn't want to remain a displaced person. Did not want to go back. We're ready come to the New World, and you couldn't come to the United States. However, they were very desperate for physicians in Bolivia. So we were going to go to Bolivia and start learning Spanish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: About a month before we were supposed to go to Bolivia, we get an affidavit that, based upon a preference quota for physicians, we could come to the United States. So now shift gears. I have to now learn English. So my father had to learn English. And my sister and I took a few lessons in English. And I remember the first thing I learned in English was about Beatrix Potter tales. In 1947, in June, we left Switzerland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We traveled to Paris, and we spent a few days in Paris with-- and we stayed as a guest of Mr. Havas, the one was in the film business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to London and stayed with one of my father's classmates from school, who was a dentist and was living in the West End. And then we took a ship called the a ship on the Polish registry going under the Belgian flag, and we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: It took eight days to cross the Atlantic. I thought I was on such a huge ship. When we docked next to the we looked like a tugboat compared to her. I mean, it was a terribly, terribly small ship in comparison. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was a wonderful trip. Food? I hadn't had that much food and never plentiful food ever in my life, as I recalled. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived to New York on a very steamy, muggy, horrible, typical New York summer day on July 16, 1947, and we arrived very early in the morning. Coming from Switzerland, we did not have to go through-- I don't even know if Ellis Island was still operating then. We did not have to go to Ellis Island. The-- the people from the Department of Health came on board, immigration people came on board. Took quite a while to get off. But the interesting thing was that I saw the West Side Highway and so many automobiles. I mean, you know, I was a seven-year-old kid. And in Europe, there were very, very few cars. There was very little fuel. And the number of automobiles was just-- just mind-boggling. And it looked like cockroaches or mice coming down the West Side Highway in the early, early, early part of the morning. And, of course, there was a terrible thunderstorm and rain, so it was overcast for a long time. So even while the day had begun, not only at 6:00 or 7:00, but about 8, 9 o'clock in the morning, the height of rush hour, it was amazing. We got off the boat finally. And everybody wore hats in those days. So Mr. Havas was there waiting for us with a straw hat. Another woman, a Mrs. Hecht, H-E-C-H-T, who was in a-- who was in the same barrack with us on top, on the third barrack-- on the third bed and high up, who had come to the United States early, she was there waiting for us, one of my mother's cousins, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're very hungry, because it had been quite some time. And the only way I could speak with Mr. Havas was in Hungarian. So I start speaking Hungarian to him about how hungry I was. And all of a sudden, here's this man, a vendor, who's selling, in Hungarian said, how would you like to have a ham sandwich? I mean, I can't believe this. What a wonderful country. Everybody speaks Hungarian here. Mr. Havas spoke Hungarian, and this vendor spoke Hungarian. And-- Were you really excited when you were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, it was-- I mean, it was a-- you know, I was so seasick for two days, but after that it was just the most marvelous thing. The crossing the-- the Atlantic was wonderful. And of course, seeing the Statue of Liberty, it didn't mean anything to me. I was a seven-year-old kid. Didn't mean any-- now it means a lot to me. But [? personally, ?] it didn't mean-- the buildings. I mean, I had seen the film with Joel Grey, I thought there were cowboys. I mean, really, you know, there were cowboys and, you know, this is nowadays, and there were cowboys and [? people, ?] horses. I also saw other films thinking that all of the United States was noontime down on Wall Street area, with thousands of people walking. These were the preceptions that I had coming to the United States, obviously. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we moved to Queens, one and two-family-- one and two-story buildings. You know, totally, totally, totally different when we came here. We stayed in the Bronx for three days, the first three days with some friends. And then we moved to Queens in three days after we'd been here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. When you went to school, you had to learn English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, of course, I started school again, and this time I started in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: The time before it had been French.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I had a lot of problems. In those days, young boys didn't wear slacks. Wore shorts-- wore shorts in the summertime, wore shorts in the wintertime. And of course, only sissies wear shorts here, or so it seems in 1947. So I was in a fight every day for the first couple months that I was in school. But by that time, I spoke pretty good English quite well, because we came in July, and school started in September. And in two months, you know, I managed to get a working vocabulary and speak the language relatively well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it more difficult for your parents to learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, it was, it was. My father-- well, firstly, my father, being a professional, he had to take a-- learn English. There was a special English for-- examination you had to pass as a foreign professional. Once you pass that before, then you could first study and take an examination for your specialty, if you were a physician or dentist or attorney, what have you. It was very, very difficult for them. My mother had been a prima donna, the daughter of very, very wealthy, as I told you, very, very wealthy parents. My father was, by the time he got married, was a rather successful pediatrician. And I told you we had three in help. So she didn't-- she didn't boil water. But when we were in Switzerland, Ort, O-R-T, taught her how to cook, how to sew, how to do all these things. And here he is-- she was the prima donna. For three cents a buttonhole she was making, but she went to work making buttonholes in a-- in a sweatshop when we first came here in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because your father wasn't able to work--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when he first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father went to study. Once he passed his test, then he worked as an intern. $50 a month they paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even then it was very little money. And slowly, slowly [INAUDIBLE]. My father never wanted to go back. We gave up our Yugoslav citizenship, and we became American citizens. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in contradistinction to many of our friends and co-commiserators, my dad never wanted anything from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got our lives, you know, whereas a lot of people-- we lost a lot, an awful lot, but never asked for any repatriation or anything like that. And let's put our past behind. Let's not forget, and let's go forward. You said your--

speaker: interviewee
text: We are now Americans, and we're going to be Americans. And we're going to live as Americans. We're going to be Jews and proud Jews, but we're going to be Americans with a central European heritage.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that when you were young, your father was a Zionist. How was his--

speaker: interviewee
text: He always was a Zionist.

speaker: interviewer
text: He always was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my mother did not want to go and live in Palestine. In 1934, they went to Palestine on their honeymoon. At the same time, her father was exhibiting at the Near East Fair in the fairground, which is now still the fairgrounds in-- in Tel Aviv. And I mean, it was just sand, not dirt, sand. It was just beach and sand and heat and the flies and-- and my mother said no. She's never going to live there in no-- no way, shape, or form. That was in '34. By '47, she had two children, and we came here because it was now impossible to go. And she said, well, we're going to make our life over here. Now, the rest of our family that survived, her two brothers, my aunt , and my maternal grandmother, the first chance that Tito let the Jews go from Yugoslavia to Israel, they immigrated to Israel. My wife's another story. My wife also went to Israel with her family. They went back to Yugoslavia and-- because they were searching, I guess, for her grandparents. And also, her father wanted to go back, and he was a pharmacist. They went back. And then in '48, they went on the first ship that allowed to take children, because she had a young brother who was just born.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so on the first ship that took children, they went, and they went to Israel. And although we had known each other as children, of course she didn't remember me. She was two years old, and I was seven. And I-- of course I didn't remember her. But when I was in Israel for the first time in 1965, I was driving in an automobile with my mother and one of her cousins. And I said, you know, I know that couple. And it was my future father-in-law and his wife. You know, I-- I had remembered them. And yes, these are the [INAUDIBLE] et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. And nothing was said and done. And then we were-- they invited us to come over for coffee and some cake. We went to the house. And of course, they had known each other very, very well. And I saw a picture of my wife, who was working for El Al at the time. And she was-- you could not fly through the Sinai-- or the Suez or what have you. So they had to go to Iran and take a base in East Africa, because they were flying to South Africa. So my wife, they would-- they would send you for six weeks there. So she was stationed six weeks in-- in Kenya at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, well, why don't you come, you know, jokingly, come visit when she comes to New York? And so she flies there. Why don't you come look me up? She came and looked me up, and here we are, 30 years, husband and wife. Wow. That's an amazing story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.
